text: The video shows a female patient with a right-sided direct inguinal hernia, evidenced by a small scar on the right side from a previous surgery.
The surgeon introduces a laparoscopic camera through the supraumbilical port, using a Veress needle for initial access.

The patient is positioned supine for the procedure. The surgeon uses an Ethicon Veress needle, which features a color indicator that turns red when the tip is pressed and green when the tip is extended. A visible ball on the needle is designed to prevent gas leakage during insufflation.

The surgeon performs the hanging drop test with suction irrigation, observing the ball floating in the fluid to confirm a positive result. The varus needle is inserted at a ninety-degree angle to the abdominal wall and a forty-five-degree angle to the patient's body. The abdominal wall is lifted, and a syringe with saline is used to perform the irrigation test, ensuring the fluid flows freely.

The surgeon performs an irrigation test followed by a suction test, observing bubbles indicating a successful suction test. The hanging drop test is conducted by lifting the abdominal wall, allowing fluid to enter, confirming a positive result. Tubing is attached to the insufflator to create pneumoperitoneum, with a set pressure of fifteen and a flow rate of one liter per minute. The actual pressure is observed to increase slowly, and liver dullness is visibly obliterated.

The surgeon taps the liver dullness area, producing a tympanic sound similar to a ripe watermelon, indicating successful pneumoperitoneum. The incision is slightly enlarged, and the first trocar is introduced through the supraumbilical port for the ipsilateral approach.

The surgeon introduces a 30-degree telescope through the supraumbilical port, with white balancing and focusing already completed. Gas is visibly escaping, accompanied by a hissing sound, as the valve is opened. The light from the telescope is seen transilluminating through the skin, highlighting the hernia defect where the muscle is absent and the skin is pushed by the hernia sac. Two epsilon ports are positioned according to the baseball diamond concept.
The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to pull the omentum out of the hernia sac, which is not adhered and easily removed. The direct hernia is visible, along with the inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon identifies the medial umbilical ligament situated between the medial and lateral umbilical ligaments, confirming a direct right-sided inguinal hernia. The light cable is adjusted to enhance visibility of the anatomical structures. The median umbilical ligament, representing the obliterated urachus, and the medial umbilical ligament, representing the obliterated umbilical artery, are noted. The lateral umbilical ligament is associated with the inferior epigastric vessel. The left side is examined, showing no hernia, and the deep ring is visible. The bladder appears slightly filled, and the uterus is observed to be multiparous and slightly bulky but without visible pathology.

The surgeon identifies the ovary and fallopian tube, then pushes small bubbles upward by tilting the patient's head 30 degrees down. The right ureter is visible with peristalsis. The sigmoid colon is gently moved to the left to expose the sacral promontory and median sacral vessels. The round ligament is seen extending to the deep ring, and the triangle of doom is identified, containing the external iliac artery and vein.
The surgeon moves the peritoneum to reveal the sacral promontory, with the median sacral vessel visible in the midline. The distance between the median sacral vessel and the ureter is observed.

The right ureter is visible, measuring approximately 2.5 centimeters, with noticeable peristalsis. The anterior longitudinal ligament is also visible, typically used for sacral colpopexy or rectopexy. The anatomy of the sacral promontory and presacral area is clearly displayed.

The surgeon identifies the external cardiac vein, cecum, and omentum. The tinea coli is visible on the cecum, and the base of the appendix is observed, extending retrocecal. The tip of the appendix is not visible, and caution is advised against manipulating the tip to avoid inducing appendicitis.

The video shows the triangle of pain, located lateral to the triangle of doom, with visible nerves including the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) and genitofemoral nerve (GFN). The shiny white area indicates the nerve region. The direct inguinal hernia is visible between the inferior epigastric vessel and medial umbilical ligament, appearing as a small, recurrent black hole. The bladder is visible and slightly distended, with no hernia observed on the left side.

On the left side, the surgeon identifies all ligaments and places an ipsilateral port lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. The procedure begins with creating a pre-peritoneal pocket by stretching the peritoneum inferior medially and making a nick using harmonic scissors.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum, allowing gas to enter the pre-peritoneal space. The lower edge of the punctured peritoneum is grasped, and the surgeon uses the tip of the harmonic or scissors to cut towards the medial umbilical ligament. The peritoneum is stretched inferior medially to prevent injury to the inferior epigastric vessel, and the medial umbilical ligament is identified before creating the lateral pocket.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection followed by medial dissection using blunt instruments, including small atraumatic graspers. The peritoneum is pushed inferomedially while the transversal fascia is separated from the peritoneum. The dissection focuses on the area of the triangle of pain, ensuring no bleeding or buttonholes are created in the peritoneum.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, identifying the spermatic vessels, which mark the medial boundary of the triangle of pain and the lateral boundary of the triangle of doom. The lateral pocket is being created.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection in the anterior peritoneum area, avoiding the use of energy to prevent nerve injury. The arc of the transversus abdominis is visible as the peritoneum is carefully separated. The lateral dissection is complete, and attention is now directed towards the medial dissection, with the medial umbilical ligament being stretched.

The surgeon carefully hooks into the groove of the space of the radius, progressing inferomedially. The Cooper's ligament, referred to as the 'lighthouse,' is identified in front of the visible hernia sac. The surgeon avoids manipulating the sac at this stage, focusing instead on medial dissection to clearly define the sac's boundary. Gradual separation of tissues reveals the Cooper's ligament.

The shiny Cooper ligament becomes visible as the surgeon manipulates the hernia sac, which covers the area in direct hernia cases. The surgeon uses instruments to feel the Cooper ligament and proceeds with dissection into the space of radius, moving towards the medial umbilical ligament. The mesh placement may cross the midline, necessitating further medial dissection.
The surgeon performs sac dissection, carefully separating the peritoneum of the hernia sac from the transversalis fascia using blunt dissection techniques.

The surgeon identifies the neck of the hernia sac and the shiny, pearly white transversalis fascia. The transversalis fascia is visible and should not be pulled; instead, the peritoneum is carefully separated from it. The sac, containing some fat and possibly lipoma, is gradually pulled out while the pseudosac is pushed back into the defect.

The surgeon has successfully removed the hernia sac, leaving the transversalis fascia pushed back into the defect. The sac is everted, resembling an inverted pocket, confirming its complete removal. The Cooper's ligament is now clearly visible below, alongside the tendinous arc of the levator ani muscle.

The obturator nerve is visible in the surgical field, along with the bladder in the space of Retzius. The surgeon has completed the removal of the hernia sac and is performing lateral dissection to enlarge the pocket, ensuring the mesh placement will not crumple. The Cooper ligament and pubic symphysis bone are clearly visible, indicating successful exposure of the surgical area.

The surgeon displays the Cooper ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster within the surgical field. The pocket is complete, and the lateral transversus abdominis is visible. A simple proline mesh is prepared for placement.

The surgeon folds the mesh into a compact shape, resembling a plate or shari, and uses a grasper to hold it obliquely. The tip of the grasper remains outside as the mesh is carefully inserted into the cannula. The valve is opened to facilitate the mesh's entry.

The surgeon opens a 15 by 12 cm mesh and uses two graspers to spread it within the pocket, ensuring the inferomedial end covers the Cooper ligament. The mesh is positioned so its center aligns with the center of the defect, with careful adjustment to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon spreads the mesh over the surgical area, ensuring coverage of the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The mesh is positioned more medially for indirect hernia repair, with its center aligned over the defect. A surgical tacker is used to secure the mesh medially.

The surgeon fixes the mesh over the Cooper ligament and spreads it laterally, securing it above the inguinal ligament over the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle. The fixation prevents mesh migration, with additional fixation points placed lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel. The center of the mesh aligns with the center of the defect, ensuring proper coverage.

The surgeon plans to perform continuous suturing using the Dundee jamming knot technique, taking bites on the anterior and posterior peritoneum. The knot is passed through the loop, allowing it to automatically lock.

The surgeon takes a bite of the anterior peritoneum and another bite on the posterior peritoneum over the medial umbilical ligament. The needle is passed through the loop, and the suture is pulled through, creating a self-sliding and self-locking knot.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the anterior peritoneum, taking bites on both the upper and lower edges. The sutures are kept slightly loose, allowing for later adjustment. The medial end is sutured first, followed by lateral fixation.

Never mind at the end you will see the magic. And again it is started. Here we can see the sack is. Making a pouch there, this sack was first in the defect. Many people they cut it, they take a rudder snot or pre-tied loop and they cut this sack out. But we don't need to do that. You know, peritoneum is a very intelligent structure God has made in our body and it has a great capacity of transmigration and metamorphosis if petronium will get immediately it will get shrink and it will get a become flat within few days you don't need to think about that petronium as we know that it can regenerate also it can reshrink also and if it is loose it will automatically get stretched or shrinked it is up to up to the structure and it is very intelligent also it knows where to cover where not to cover And where to regenerate, how much to regenerate. So you don't worry about. And some people they fix it. Of course you can do that but not necessary. Especially for a small size of the sack it is not necessary.

The surgeon performs continuous suturing using an endoske needle, pressing it against the bowel surface with the upper jaw to facilitate the suturing process. The sutures are placed at approximately centimeter intervals, ensuring they remain loose at this stage.

The surgeon takes a bite on the lateral edge with the needle, followed by a deeper bite in the undissected area, pulling the suture laterally towards the upper abdomen. The peritoneum closes automatically as the suture is pulled parallel to the abdominal wall, ensuring it does not cut through. The insufflation pressure is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury.

The surgeon completes the peritonealization, ensuring no part of the mesh is visible. They perform an Aberdeen termination by creating a loop within a loop, pulling the needle through to lock the suture. The non-needle end is pulled to secure the knot, completing the closure of the peritoneum.

The surgeon completes the closure of the peritoneum using sutures, followed by cutting the suture with scissors. The entire peritoneum is visibly closed, and the needle is removed, marking the completion of the transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair in a female patient.
